sentences.text: Richard Ludwig Pick.
My mother's maiden name was Baum.
Pick is P-I-C-K-- P as in 'Peter,' I, C as in 'Charles,' K as in 'kilo.'
Yes, it was.
No, no, I never had, no.
Well, Hebrew name, yes, I had a Hebrew name.
You cannot become bar mitzvah without a Hebrew name.
It is [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Josef ben Reubenheim.
No, I'm sorry-- Reuben ben Josefheim.
I was born in Solingen, Baden, in Germany, which is near Karlsruhe, on July 25, 1921.
75-- I will be in July.
Yes, well, in Solingen, my father had a, a, a spinning mill.
My father, then-- when he got married, he was a well-to-do man.
And he had a spinning mill.
What he actually did-- he bought old uniforms.
This was after the First World War, right after the war.
He bought truckloads and trainloads of old uniforms of the German soldiers and of the French Army, which were washed and torn up and finally spun into, into yarn.
Because there was a great shortage of everything.
Then from, from Solingen, we moved to [GERMAN], where the inflation started.
In 1922, I was a year old.
And between 1922 and 1924, my father lost a good part of what he had made before in the inflation.
It was a hyperinflation.
And from there, we moved to Karlsruhe.
In Karlsruhe, my brother was born.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father bought a department store in Offenbach, which was a bankrupt department store he bought relatively cheap.
But he never could make a go of it, either.
So from there, we went to Stuttgart.
Mm-hmm.
That's where my mother was from.
My father was born in Freudenstadt, which is in the Black Forest.
My mother was from Stuttgart.
And there he started a very-- a little shop.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then he opened another one in a town by the name of Backnang, which is north of Stuttgart.
And he was doing all right-- not very well.
And well, we had a childhood like most people [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in-- on our economic level.
We went to school in Stuttgart.
Larry, my brother, and myself, we went to grammar school.
From grammar school, we went to-- I went to the Karls-Gymnasium.
I learned Latin and Greek.
I wanted to become a physician, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] which, of course, was never achieved because I was thrown out of school in 1936, being Jewish.
Conservative.
They were conservative.
My, my grandfather was a, relatively speaking, a religious man.
He, he didn't smoke on the Shabbat.
They, they had two different types of dishes.
And they were religious, yes, my grandma and my grandfather.
But my mother, she kept also a religious household.
But it was not as strict as in my grandfather's home.
The dishes got mingled.
We never ate pork.
We never ate milchig and fleishig together.
But it was-- I wouldn't call it a, a very kosher household, no.
Yes.
We went, besides-- I mean, once I went to the gymnasium, then we had also religious school.
We had religious school.
While the, the Protestant and Catholic children had their religious classes, once or twice a week, we had ours.
There was a Jewish teacher coming to the Karls-Gymnasium, to the school.
And he taught us mainly history-- Jewish history, Bible.
And then a little bit later, maybe two years later, we all just start taking Hebrew lessons, also in school.
Well--
Well, we felt-- I would say, at that  age, you don't know what's happening.
All of a sudden, I mean-- you mean before Hitler?
Before Hitler, I must say that we felt-- we knew we were Jews quite well.
But we also felt that we were Germans.
Many of us felt that they were Germans first and then Jews-- I would say most of us.
They were very proud to be Germans.
I beg your pardon?
Oh, yes, I had two uncles who fought in World War I and who got killed in World War I.
In fact, one is still missing.
And the other one is buried in Stuttgart.
He was-- his body was taken from Poland by his father, my grandfather, from Poland to Stuttgart.
And he's buried in the Jewish cemetery in Stuttgart.
Yes, he's buried right across from where my, my grandfather is buried.
The Jewish community, there were about 2,000 heads.
They had a beautiful synagogue.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We went to a, a synagogue, of course, on all the holidays.
And we also went, as children, to the-- they had a, a service for children-- a children's service Saturday afternoons.
And we went to-- many times we went to that children's service.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] We felt-- we felt-- we knew we were Jews.
But we had many, many-- I mean, most of our friends, I would say, especially in my case, were gentiles, were Christian until the moment of-- Hitler came to power.
The first two years, they were still friends, not as intimate anymore as they were before.
But then, in the-- at the beginning of 1936, it was a completely-- a break off.
I mean, they didn't even look at you anymore.
For me, it was terrible, especially-- I remember one occasion I was a-- I loved to play soccer.
It was part of my life.
[CRYING] And [CRYING] I played in the varsity team of the school.
Yes, even though I was only 16 years old.
Because most of the, the team players on the team were 17.
And some of them were already 18 years old.
So we made it finally.
And we got into the final against the-- this was an inter-city tournament which they had every year, or every two years.
And we got into the final.

And just a week or two weeks before the final [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was supposed to be played-- or was played, the instructor, the physical-- of-- the instructor, he came around and said, Richard, I have to talk to you.
His name was Kugelberger.
And he said, Richard, I am sorry, but you can't play.
[CRYING] It was for a 16-years-old.
This was in 1936, yes.
Then I was kicked out of school.
So was my brother.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my parents, since my father was a Czech-- he had a Czech passport.
But he didn't speak a word of Czech.
But since his father was Czech and had a Czech passport-- he didn't speak a word of Czech, either, his father.
But he was born in Freudenstadt.
And in Germany, there is a law-- and there still is, I think-- that wherever-- whatever your father is at your birth, this is the nationality you take.
So my father was Czech.
And I was Czech.
And my father took advantage.
He went to the Czech Consulate.
And he got a Czech passport.
And we immigrated to Czechoslovakia in 1937--
--to the Sudetenland, to a town by the name of Teplitz-Schonau.
But unfortunately, I couldn't go to continue-- I was supposed to continue my studies.
But at that time, there was a big fight going on between the, the Germans living in the Sudetenland and the Czechs living in the Sudetenland.
And they, they brought out new laws that there were no-- it was not bilingual anymore in the schools.
But it was 100% Czech.
So I was supposed to pass my examination in Czech within six weeks, which, of course, was impossible.
So my father said, you better go back to, to Stuttgart.
And he corresponded with my grandmother.
And my grandmother was very happy that I was coming back.
I lived with my grandmother.
And my parents lived in Teplitz-Schonau until the Sudetenland was invaded by the German Army, which was in 1938.
Mm-hmm.
I'd like to get back briefly to the time 1933 to '36, before you left.
Can you talk some about the changes you started to see with Hitler's rise to power?
You said people would stop talking to you who were former friends.
Is there any other experiences you had personally with-- or feelings that you had?
Did you have conversations with your parents?
Well, the feeling actually was that, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at that age, I felt like an outcast.
I felt like an outcast.
And sometimes-- you know, the propaganda in Germany was very strong.
Goebbels, he was a very good propaganda minister.
And they were repeating and repeating everything.
And in school, too, you know, they were singing songs against the Jews.
So-- [SIGHS]
No, I don't think I remember the words of, of, of-- you remember some songs, yes?
Yes, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
Well, this we didn't sing in school.
But they sang it on the street.
[SPEAKING GERMAN]--
'When Jewish blood'--
Yes, 'burst from the-- from the knives,' yes.
And so I was very much impressed by that German propaganda.
Sometimes-- don't forget that-- I was, at the time, I was 16 years old.
They were all going into the Hitler Youth.
They were-- the kids, they were walking around in uniforms.
And I, of course, didn't go.
I should say, I couldn't go, you know, to the-- to those meetings.
It was like the Boy Scouts, but of course very much to the right.
And I always-- I almost envied these fellow schoolmates, you know.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I felt very bad about it.
And then reading the papers like and-- I mean, we just glanced at it, you know.
It was all anti-Jewish propaganda.
And sometimes I didn't know whether they were right or whether we were right, you know?
I mean, it just impressed me so much at that time.
Mm-hmm.
Then later on, of course, things changed.
But it, it made a big impression on me.
It, it affected my self-esteem.
It affected my, my ego.
No, I was never personally threatened.
Nor were my parents, at that time, personally threatened.
That was still relatively early in the game.
Things got bad in 1938, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] during and after the Crystal Night--
--November 9, 1938.
Now until 1938, I-- there was-- I couldn't go to school anymore.
So I went to the-- I became an apprentice in a garment factory.
It was a large garment factory in Stuttgart.
[GERMAN] was the name, in the Alte Strasse, Alte Street, number 2.
And that company was-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] had to be sold to, to Christians because Jews-- they gave them a certain amount of time to get rid of their businesses.
Of course, all of those businesses were sold under, under pressure.
And the owners got relatively very little money on account of this.
So that business, when it was taken over, well, I was thrown out there, too.
--out of the gymnasium.
Then I went as an apprentice in-- I went to Czechoslovakia.
I came back to my grandmother.
I went to the [GERMAN] as an apprentice.
Then I was thrown out of [GERMAN], the, the dress factory.
And then I went to a, a men's garment factory, Gebruder [GERMAN], in Stuttgart, also.
They made men's suits and coats in the Paulinenstrasse, Paulinen Street.
And there again, after a very few months, that was when the limit came.
I mean, you had to sell your business now.
They just sold their business at the last moment.
They got very little for it.
And then I was thrown out, too.
So there was no possibility anymore for Jews to work anyplace or to go to school.
So--
The only possibility then was to become a construction worker.
And I became a plasterer, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] working with gentile people who have been plasterers all their, their lives.
And it was on the-- on that little suburb of Stuttgart, in Feuerbach.
The company's name was, was A.
[NON-ENGLISH] and Company.
And there I worked inside buildings, building factories, outside the buildings, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] helping to paint the buildings, too, sometimes.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then there was-- and, and, and I was told that an organization by the name of Judische Auswandererstelle [SPEAKING GERMAN] was being created.
And they were looking for people-- for all kinds of people.
So I applied.
I mean, I, I knew I didn't have much of a chance because there were many people who were applying.
But my advantage was in one of the summer vacations, my mother had sent me to learn how to type and how to take shorthand.
And I became very good at it.
So they gave me a test, which came out very good.
And I was employed.
That organization actually was created by German authorities.
It was created about a, a year-- no, less than a year, maybe six or eight months after the Crystal Night, after November 9, 1938.
It was created in 1939 for the purpose to take-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to help Jews to comply with their different papers.
There was a lot of paperwork involved if you wanted to emigrate.
You needed all kinds of permits from all kinds of German authorities, from the police department, from the [SPEAKING GERMAN].
You needed an authorization-- what you could take along and what you couldn't take along.
There were laws.
You had to pay what they called a Reichsfluchsteuer.
That means you had to pay a tax because you were fleeing from Germany.
That's why they called it Reichsfluchsteuer.
So all this-- we helped the people with-- to make the corresponding papers and to fill in the papers.
And I worked with the, the [GERMAN].
And so I had the opportunity to go to these different German authorities twice or three times a week.
In fact, before I, I emigrated-- every Jew had to go to the Gestapo, too.
That was the final OK you had to get from the Gestapo, which was a guy in the Staatspolizei, the secret police of the state.
And you had to get the OK.
And when I finally emigrated, I had to go in there, too, for myself now, to get the OK for myself to, to emigrate.
With all the other papers, you had to present it there.
They, they looked them over.
And while you were standing there at attention, they, they put a stamp on it.
Or they said, no.
Or they said, yes.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- we tried to get out of Germany.
If I say, 'we,' there were a few friends.
Some of them got lost on the way.
They were in someplace else-- they-- within Germany.
They had heard that it would be cheaper-- not cheaper.
It would be easier to emigrate if you would go to Dresden.
Or if you would go to, to the Rhineland, you might be able to get to, to [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Holland or to Belgium.
But then later on, Holland and Belgium, of course, they were also invaded by the-- by the German Armies.
So in 1941, I was still working for the Auswandererstelle.
This is actually when I emigrated.
But there's a little story to that immigration.
Can I go back for a moment to-- you said in 1938-- you mentioned Kristallnacht.
Yes.
Well, the Kristallnacht, I, I remember it quite well, yes.
I-- in fact, Lore, my wife, she was standing there while this synagogue was burning.
I got there when the firemen came to extinguish the fire.
But of course, they came when [LAUGHS] the synagogue was burned down.
So there wasn't much standing there anymore when I got there.
I mean, it was pretty much burned out.
It was burning a-- it was just a, a, a pile-- a pile of rubble.
And just some of the, the outer parts were still standing.
And how did you know it was happening?
Or what, what brought--
Well, it was the next day.
It was now November 10th, you know.
And they, they-- I mean, if you read the paper, and when you-- they, they, they said that German-- the German hate for the Jews brought this about.
But it-- of, of course, it was all an organized-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it was organized beforehand, very well organized, because it was done all over Germany at the same time.
They, they smashed the windows of all Jewish shops.
Of course, a lot was-- of the merchandise was plundered.
They took-- the next day and during that particular night, they took-- all Jewish men, they took to Dachau, which was a concentration camp near Munich.
And some people, they never got out of Dachau.
Those who got out of Dachau were told-- and I know this from my uncle, who had been in Dachau.
I was not in Dachau because they did not-- they did not take any-- anyone below the age of 18.
So when my uncle came back about two or three months after November-- it must have been January or February of, of 1939-- he told me that they were told-- and they had to sign.
They were told by the Germans there-- and they had to sign the corresponding papers that they will try their best to get out of Germany, to emigrate.

And this was the reason then, too, that the Gestapo, the German secret police, established that Judische Auswandererstelle to, to have a, a, a contact, a liaison between the, the potential immigrants and the German authorities.
And--
Where I worked, yes.
